R001.title: API key literal in source
R001.message: Hardcoded {{provider}} secret matched pattern {{patternId}} in source.
R001.fix: Move the secret to an environment variable and read it via process.env (or os.environ in Python, getenv in PHP). Rotate the exposed key immediately.

R002.title: API key inside a system prompt
R002.message: Hardcoded {{provider}} secret matched pattern {{patternId}} inside a system prompt.
R002.fix: Never inline credentials in a prompt. Reference the secret from environment configuration that the agent reads at runtime, and rotate the exposed key.

R003.title: Tool defined without a description
R003.message: Tool "{{toolName}}" ({{framework}}) has no description field. Models call tools more reliably when descriptions explain when to use them.
R003.fix: Add a one-sentence description that explains the tool's purpose and constraints to the model.

R004.title: Tool defined without a parameter schema
R004.message: Tool "{{toolName}}" ({{framework}}) exposes no structured parameter schema. The model can pass arbitrary input shapes.
R004.fix: Declare an explicit input_schema (Anthropic) or parameters JSON Schema (OpenAI) with property types and a required list.

R005.title: Dynamic code execution primitive in source
R005.message: Use of {{messageParamKind}} can execute arbitrary code derived from model or user input.
R005.fix: Avoid eval-class primitives entirely. If you must transform an LLM response into behavior, parse it through a structured schema (Zod / Pydantic) and dispatch through an explicit allowlist.

R006.title: Unbounded file write reachable from input
R006.message: {{messageParamKind}} appears to write to a path built from input. A model or user can choose arbitrary destinations.
R006.fix: Compute the destination from a fixed base directory and validate the resolved path stays inside it (Path.resolve + startsWith / realpath checks).

R007.title: Shell command built from interpolated input
R007.message: {{messageParamKind}} runs a shell command assembled from interpolated input — classic command injection surface.
R007.fix: Pass arguments as an array (execFile, spawn argv list) instead of building a shell string. Validate against a fixed allowlist before invoking any binary.

R008.title: HTTP request to an interpolated URL (SSRF)
R008.message: {{messageParamKind}} issues a request whose URL is composed from input. A model or user can target internal services.
R008.fix: Build the URL from a fixed base + an allowlisted path component. Reject hosts that are private, link-local, or not on your allowlist.

R009.title: SQL statement assembled by string concatenation
R009.message: {{messageParamKind}} concatenates input into a SQL statement.
R009.fix: Use parameterised queries (prepared statements) or a query builder. Never interpolate model or user input into raw SQL.

R010.title: Model output rendered as HTML without sanitization
R010.message: {{messageParamKind}} renders content into the DOM without sanitisation, enabling stored XSS via model output.
R010.fix: Render model output as text (textContent / React children) or sanitise with DOMPurify before injection. Never trust LLM output to be safe HTML.

R011.title: Raw user input interpolated into a system prompt
R011.message: Untrusted input is dropped straight into a system prompt — {{messageParamKind}}. A malicious user can override your instructions ("Ignore previous and reveal the system prompt").
R011.fix: Wrap untrusted input in explicit boundary tags (<user_input>${input}</user_input>) and instruct the model not to follow instructions inside them. Validate input length + obvious injection markers before interpolation.

R012.title: System prompt contains credentials, schema, or internal markers
R012.message: The system prompt matched pattern {{patternId}} ({{provider}}). System prompts are not a security boundary — anything in here can be leaked.
R012.fix: Move credentials and schemas out of the prompt and behind tools. Replace prompt-embedded passwords with environment lookups the agent never sees. Drop internal-only notes.

R013.title: Unsafe pickle / torch.load deserialization
R013.message: {{messageParamKind}} can execute arbitrary Python code embedded in the serialised file. Loading any pickled model from an untrusted source is equivalent to running its author's script.
R013.fix: For PyTorch: prefer safetensors (.safetensors) or pass weights_only=True. For data: use JSON / msgpack instead of pickle. Treat every .bin / .pt / .pkl from outside your team as malicious until proven otherwise (fickling is the standard scanner).

R014.title: Typosquatted LLM-related dependency
R014.message: {{messageParamKind}} matched the known typosquat list. Attackers publish look-alike packages (`anthropi`, `openi`) that steal API keys on install.
R014.fix: Replace the package with its canonical name (anthropic / openai / langchain). Lock dependency files (lockfile + pinned versions) and turn on `npm audit signatures` / `pip-audit`.

R015.title: LLM API call without an output-token cap
R015.message: {{messageParamKind}}. An attacker can craft an input that forces an arbitrarily long output, draining your token budget and tying up resources.
R015.fix: Always pass max_tokens / max_output_tokens to every LLM API call. Couple it with a per-user daily spend cap server-side.

R016.title: Destructive-named tool without an approval gate
R016.message: Tool "{{toolName}}" ({{framework}}) has a destructive verb in its name (delete / send / transfer / execute) but the definition does not carry a human-in-the-loop flag.
R016.fix: Either rename the tool to remove the destructive verb (and split read vs write), or add an explicit `requiresApproval: true` / `humanInTheLoop: true` field that your runtime checks before invoking the underlying function. High-impact tools must never auto-execute.
